content.text: A private cloud is a cloud computing environment dedicated exclusively to a single organization. Unlike the public cloud — where physical hardware is shared across thousands of customers and isolated only in software — private cloud infrastructure is either owned outright by the organization or leased on a single-tenant basis. The defining property is exclusivity: no other organization's workloads run on the same physical machines. AWS supports private cloud deployments through AWS Outposts (bringing AWS hardware on-premises), Dedicated Hosts, Dedicated Instances, and the AWS GovCloud regions.
What Makes a Cloud "Private"?
Three properties distinguish private cloud from public cloud:
Single tenancy.
Physical compute, storage, and networking resources serve only one organization. There is no hypervisor-level or network-level co-tenancy with external parties.
Dedicated hardware.

Whether the hardware lives in your data center or in a colocation facility, specific servers are allocated to your organization's exclusive use. This enables hardware-level compliance attestation and eliminates any theoretical cross-tenant attack surface at the physical layer.
Data residency control.

When private cloud infrastructure is on-premises, the organization has physical custody of its data. Data does not traverse a provider's network or reside in a provider's facility — satisfying the strictest data-sovereignty and air-gap requirements.
Private Cloud Architecture with AWS Outposts

attrs.alt: Private Cloud architecture diagram showing AWS Outposts rack in customer data center connected via Direct Connect to AWS Region for management and burst capacity
attrs.title: Private Cloud — AWS Outposts and Connectivity Model

content.text: With AWS Outposts, AWS ships a rack of hardware to your data center, installs and maintains it, and connects it back to an AWS Region over a dedicated Service Link. The compute and storage run locally — data never leaves your premises — while the AWS control plane in the region handles management, software updates, and API compatibility. You use the same AWS Console, CLI, and SDKs for Outposts resources as for regular EC2 instances.
AWS Private Cloud Options
AWS offers several products for organizations that need varying degrees of physical isolation or on-premises presence:
Option
Where hardware lives
Who manages HW
Best for
AWS Outposts Rack
Your data center / colocation
AWS (AWS-owned hardware)
Data residency, low-latency local processing, air-gap adjacent
Outposts Servers (1U/2U)
Your data center / edge location
AWS
Smaller footprint; factory floors, retail, hospitals
Dedicated Hosts
AWS data center (in a Region)
AWS
BYOL (bring-your-own-license) software, compliance requiring physical server audit
Dedicated Instances
AWS data center (in a Region)
AWS
Logical single-tenancy; no workloads from other accounts on same host
AWS GovCloud (US)
AWS data center (US only)
AWS
US federal, ITAR, FedRAMP High, DoD IL2–IL5 workloads
AWS Outposts in Depth

AWS Outposts is the flagship private cloud offering: a fully managed service that extends AWS infrastructure, services, APIs, and tools to virtually any data center, colocation space, or on-premises facility. The hardware is AWS-built and AWS-owned. AWS technicians install it, monitor it remotely, replace failed components, and apply firmware and software patches — the customer never touches the Outposts hardware itself.
Service Link: The Control Plane Connection

Outposts requires a Service Link — an outbound HTTPS connection from the Outpost to the AWS Region that serves as its "home" region. The Service Link carries control plane traffic: management API calls, software updates, and monitoring telemetry.
Customer data traffic never traverses the Service Link.

All data plane traffic (EC2 instance communication, EBS I/O, RDS queries) stays entirely within the Outpost, on your premises. If the Service Link goes offline, existing workloads on the Outpost keep running; only new instance launches and control-plane operations (stopping/starting instances, changing security groups) require reconnection.
Local Gateway: Routing to On-Premises Systems

The Local Gateway (LGW) connects the Outpost's VPC subnets directly to your on-premises network. Unlike internet or region traffic, LGW-routed traffic stays on your internal network, enabling sub-millisecond latency between EC2 instances on the Outpost and legacy systems on the same LAN. This is critical for Outposts use cases where workloads need to interact with on-premises databases, manufacturing equipment, or real-time control systems.
Ordering and Setting Up an Outpost

# 1. Create an Outpost (done via AWS Console order process; this records the ARN)
# After physical installation, AWS provides the Outpost ARN:
OUTPOST_ARN="arn:aws:outposts:us-east-1:111122223333:outpost/op-0123456789abcdef0"

# 2. Create a subnet in a VPC and associate it with the Outpost
SUBNET_ID=$(aws ec2 create-subnet \
  --vpc-id vpc-0a1b2c3d \
  --cidr-block 10.0.10.0/24 \
  --outpost-arn "$OUTPOST_ARN" \
  --availability-zone us-east-1a \
  --query 'Subnet.SubnetId' --output text)

# 3. Get available instance types on the Outpost
aws ec2 describe-instance-types \
  --filters "Name=supported-placement-group-strategy,Values=partition" \
  --query 'InstanceTypes[].InstanceType' --output table

# 4. Launch an EC2 instance on the Outpost subnet (same API as region EC2)
aws ec2 run-instances \
  --image-id ami-0abcdef1234567890 \
  --instance-type m5.xlarge \
  --subnet-id "$SUBNET_ID" \
  --count 1 \
  --key-name my-key-pair

# 5. Configure Local Gateway route for on-premises traffic
LGW_ID=$(aws ec2 describe-local-gateways \
  --filters "Name=outpost-arn,Values=$OUTPOST_ARN" \
  --query 'LocalGateways[0].LocalGatewayId' --output text)

RTB_ID=$(aws ec2 describe-route-tables \
  --filters "Name=vpc-id,Values=vpc-0a1b2c3d" \
  --query 'RouteTables[0].RouteTableId' --output text)

aws ec2 create-route \
  --route-table-id "$RTB_ID" \
  --destination-cidr-block 192.168.0.0/16 \
  --local-gateway-id "$LGW_ID"
Dedicated Hosts

AWS Dedicated Hosts allocate an entire physical server in an AWS Region exclusively to your AWS account. Unlike Outposts, Dedicated Hosts run in AWS's data centers — your data still resides on AWS premises — but no other customer's virtual machines share the physical host.
The primary use case is
bring-your-own-license (BYOL)

software. Microsoft Windows Server, SQL Server, Oracle Database, and some RHEL licenses are priced per physical socket or per core. AWS Dedicated Hosts expose the socket and core count of the underlying server, allowing you to apply existing per-socket licenses rather than paying AWS's bundled license fees. The savings can be substantial for large SQL Server or Oracle deployments.

# Allocate a Dedicated Host for m5 instances (single tenant physical server)
HOST_ID=$(aws ec2 allocate-hosts \
  --availability-zone us-east-1a \
  --instance-family m5 \
  --auto-placement off \
  --quantity 1 \
  --query 'HostIds[0]' --output text)

echo "Dedicated Host: $HOST_ID"

# Launch an instance targeting that specific host
aws ec2 run-instances \
  --image-id ami-0abcdef1234567890 \
  --instance-type m5.2xlarge \
  --placement "Tenancy=host,HostId=$HOST_ID" \
  --count 1

# Release the host when no longer needed (billing stops)
# aws ec2 release-hosts --host-ids "$HOST_ID"
Benefits of Private Cloud
Physical data custody.
On-premises private cloud (Outposts) ensures that data never leaves your premises. Intelligence agencies, defense contractors, and some financial regulators require this as a hard constraint.
Regulatory and compliance requirements.

Some regulations specify where data must physically reside, or require that a certifiable chain of custody exists for the physical media. On-premises private cloud satisfies these without the complexity of air-gap networks.
Ultra-low latency to on-premises systems.

Applications that must interact with on-premises manufacturing lines, real-time trading systems, or legacy databases at sub-millisecond latency cannot tolerate the 1–5ms round-trip to the nearest AWS region. Outposts places the compute on the same LAN as the systems it talks to.
Hardware-level compliance attestation.

Dedicated Hosts provide visibility into the physical host's identity, socket count, and core count — enabling auditors to verify that software licenses are correctly applied and that no cross-tenant sharing occurred.
Consistent API and tooling with the public cloud.

AWS Outposts uses the same EC2, RDS, EKS, and ECS APIs as the public cloud. Teams trained on AWS skills can operate Outposts without re-training. CloudFormation templates and Terraform modules work against Outposts with minor subnet-targeting changes.
Trade-offs and Challenges
High upfront cost.

Outposts racks are a significant capital or leasing commitment. The minimum Outpost configuration starts at a single rack; pricing depends on the EC2 instance families ordered. Unlike the public cloud, you commit to capacity in advance rather than scaling on demand.
Physical site requirements.

Outposts racks require specific power (208V), cooling (kW of cooling capacity), networking (multiple 25 Gbps fiber uplinks), and physical space in a standard server rack. Not every data center or office environment can meet these requirements.
Smaller service catalog.

Not every AWS service is available on Outposts. The list of locally available services includes EC2, EBS, ECS, EKS, RDS, EMR, and a few others — but services like S3 object storage, Lambda, CloudFront, and most AI/ML services run only in the connected Region.
Service Link dependency for control plane.

New EC2 instance launches, security group changes, and most management operations require an active Service Link connection to the home region. Designing for Service Link outages requires extra thought — e.g., pre-launched instances that are kept running rather than stopped.
Fixed capacity.

An Outpost rack has a fixed compute and storage capacity. You cannot instantly scale beyond the rack's capacity the way public cloud EC2 Auto Scaling can. Over-provisioning wastes money; under-provisioning requires ordering additional racks with weeks of lead time.
Staffing for on-premises requirements.

Even though AWS manages the Outpost hardware remotely, your organization must provide the physical space, power, cooling, and network connectivity. Coordinating physical access for AWS technicians (for rare hardware replacements) adds operational overhead that a pure public cloud deployment eliminates.
Common Use Cases
Financial services trading systems.

Low-latency trading platforms need compute physically co-located with exchange connections or market data feeds. An Outpost placed in the same colocation facility as the exchange eliminates the 1–2ms region round-trip.
Healthcare and life sciences.

Medical imaging systems, EHR (electronic health record) platforms, and genomics pipelines may require that patient data never leave a specific hospital facility or national boundary. Outposts handles the compute while S3 on Outposts (where available) stores imaging data locally.
Manufacturing and industrial IoT.

Factory floor equipment produces sensor data that must be processed in real time with millisecond latency. Outpost Servers (1U/2U) can be placed directly in the factory to run EC2 instances that analyze sensor streams and control machinery, with processed results or aggregated metrics sent to the region.
Government and defense.

Classified workloads require data to remain within government-controlled facilities. For US federal agencies, AWS GovCloud (US) provides FedRAMP High and DoD IL2–IL5 authorization in US-person-operated data centers. For non-US governments, Outposts placed in government facilities meet equivalent requirements.
Retail point of sale.

Large retail chains run EC2 workloads on Outpost Servers at each store to process transactions locally, even when the connection to the AWS region is intermittent. Inventory, pricing, and receipt data are processed on-site and synced to the region when connectivity is available.
Shared Responsibility on AWS Outposts

The shared responsibility model shifts on Outposts compared to the public cloud. Because the hardware is in your facility, you take on physical security responsibilities that AWS handles in its own data centers:
Responsibility
Public Cloud (Region)
AWS Outposts
Physical server hardware
AWS
AWS (hardware owned and maintained by AWS)
Physical facility (building, power, cooling)
AWS
Customer
(you provide the space, power, cooling)
Physical security of rack
AWS
Customer
(you control physical access to the facility)
Hypervisor / Nitro
AWS
AWS
Guest OS (on EC2)
Customer
Customer
Network connectivity to Region
AWS
Customer
(you provision the Direct Connect or internet uplink)
Designing for Service Link Resilience
Because Outposts depends on connectivity to the home region for management operations, architects must design for scenarios where the Service Link is temporarily unavailable. The key principle is:
running instances keep running; only new control-plane operations fail.
Pre-launch instances and keep them running.

If your application must be able to scale during a disconnection event, pre-launch instances and keep them in a stopped state locally — stopped instances can be started locally without a region call. Or keep a warm pool of running instances that auto-scaling can redirect traffic to.
Use Direct Connect with redundant circuits.

The Service Link runs over the internet by default. For production Outposts, provision a Direct Connect connection from your data center to the home region. Direct Connect provides dedicated bandwidth and more predictable latency than the public internet; pairing two Direct Connect connections in different physical paths eliminates single points of failure.
Store secrets and AMIs locally.

IAM credential resolution and EC2 AMI pulls both require region connectivity at launch time. Pre-cache frequently used AMIs in a local EBS snapshot or use AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store with local caching to reduce region calls during instance startup.
When to Choose Private Cloud
Private cloud adds cost and complexity. Choose it only when a specific, documented requirement cannot be met by the public cloud:

A regulation or contract explicitly requires that data not leave your physical premises or a specific geographic jurisdiction, and a public cloud Region in that jurisdiction does not exist or does not satisfy the requirement.
Workloads require sub-millisecond latency to on-premises systems (factory automation, real-time control, co-located trading) that makes a region round-trip technically infeasible.
Legacy software licensing terms (Oracle, SQL Server per-socket) make dedicated hardware significantly cheaper than cloud-native licensing despite the private cloud's higher infrastructure cost.
Your organization needs the consistent APIs and operational model of AWS on-premises to reduce the skill gap between on-premises and cloud teams, without committing to full migration.
AWS GovCloud (US): Government Private Cloud

AWS GovCloud (US) is a pair of isolated AWS Regions (us-gov-east-1 and us-gov-west-1) designed to host sensitive US government data and regulated workloads. They are operated exclusively by US persons (US citizens and US permanent residents) who have passed background checks, are physically located in the United States, and satisfy US government personnel requirements.
GovCloud runs the same AWS services and APIs as commercial regions — EC2, S3, RDS, Lambda, EKS — but with additional controls that satisfy specific US government frameworks:
FedRAMP High.
Covers the most sensitive unclassified federal information. Required for federal agency systems that store, process, or transmit sensitive but unclassified (SBU) data.
DoD Impact Levels 2–5.

The Department of Defense Cloud Computing Security Requirements Guide (SRG) levels from public non-sensitive (IL2) through controlled unclassified information (IL5). GovCloud is authorized for IL2 through IL5 workloads on a per-service basis.
ITAR compliance.

International Traffic in Arms Regulations restrict who can access technical data related to defense articles. GovCloud's US-person restriction on operators ensures that ITAR-controlled technical data stays within the required access boundaries.
CJIS compliance.

Criminal Justice Information Services requirements for law enforcement. GovCloud's personnel controls and audit capabilities satisfy CJIS Security Policy requirements for cloud-hosted criminal justice information.

GovCloud accounts are separate from commercial AWS accounts and cannot be created directly; they must be linked to an existing commercial account. All billing still flows through the commercial account. Not all AWS services are available in GovCloud — check the GovCloud service list before designing architecture.
Monitoring Outposts with CloudWatch

CloudWatch automatically collects metrics from EC2 instances and managed services running on Outposts, but the metrics flow to the home AWS Region (not stored locally). You can create alarms and dashboards in the region that cover both Outpost and region resources in a single view. The following pattern publishes a custom metric from an Outpost-hosted application and creates an alarm for it:

import boto3

# boto3 on an Outpost EC2 instance uses the same regional endpoint
# Metrics are published to CloudWatch in the home region automatically
cw = boto3.client('cloudwatch', region_name='us-east-1')

def publish_queue_depth(depth: int, outpost_id: str) -> None:
    """Publish application queue depth as a custom metric from Outpost."""
    cw.put_metric_data(
        Namespace='MyApp/Outpost',
        MetricData=[
            {
                'MetricName': 'QueueDepth',
                'Value': depth,
                'Unit': 'Count',
                'Dimensions': [
                    {'Name': 'OutpostId', 'Value': outpost_id},
                    {'Name': 'Environment', 'Value': 'production'},
                ]
            }
        ]
    )

# Create an alarm that triggers if queue depth exceeds 1000 for 5 minutes
cw.put_metric_alarm(
    AlarmName='OutpostQueueDepthHigh',
    MetricName='QueueDepth',
    Namespace='MyApp/Outpost',
    Period=60,           # 1-minute evaluation period
    EvaluationPeriods=5, # alert after 5 consecutive periods above threshold
    Threshold=1000,
    ComparisonOperator='GreaterThanThreshold',
    Statistic='Maximum',
    AlarmActions=['arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:111122223333:ops-alerts'],
    Dimensions=[{'Name': 'OutpostId', 'Value': 'op-0123456789abcdef0'}]
)
Cost Model for Private Cloud
Private cloud costs are structured differently from public cloud pay-per-use billing. Understanding the full cost model prevents underestimating the total investment:
Outposts pricing: capacity reservation.

Outposts racks are priced based on the EC2 and EBS capacity ordered. You pay for provisioned capacity, not used capacity — an Outpost running at 20% utilization costs the same as one at 100%. Pricing is available in 3-year and 1-year terms; no hourly option exists.
Facility costs.

Power, cooling, and physical space for the rack are the customer's responsibility. A 42U rack at full load can draw 5–10 kW continuously. At $0.10/kWh, that is $4,380–$8,760/year in electricity alone, plus colocation fees if the rack is not in your own building.
Direct Connect cost.

A 1 Gbps Direct Connect port at an AWS Direct Connect location typically costs $220/month (port fee) plus the colocation cross-connect fee (~$100–300/month). Data transfer out from the home region over Direct Connect is charged per GB.
Dedicated Hosts pricing.

A Dedicated Host is priced as a per-host hourly rate or Reserved Host (1- or 3-year term). For example, an m5 Dedicated Host (on-demand) costs roughly $3.37/hour (~$2,400/month) and provides sockets and cores to run as many m5 instances as fit the host.
TCO comparison point.

The break-even point where private cloud (Outposts) is cheaper than equivalent public cloud EC2 On-Demand capacity is typically at high sustained utilization (>70%) over a 3-year term. Below that utilization level, On-Demand or Reserved Instances in the public cloud are almost always cheaper when facility and staffing costs are included in the TCO calculation.
Security Controls Specific to Outposts

Running AWS on your premises introduces security considerations that do not apply to the public cloud. You own the physical access controls and must implement them with the same rigor that AWS applies in its own data centers:
Physical rack access control.

The Outpost rack must be physically secured — keycard access, security cameras, access logging. AWS personnel access the rack only via a support case through the AWS console; no standing physical access is granted. Your security team controls who can physically touch the equipment.
Network isolation.

The uplink from the Outpost rack to your network should go through a dedicated network segment — not through the same switch fabric as unsecured production or office traffic. Use dedicated VLANs and physical network separation to prevent lateral movement from less-trusted network zones.
Service Link security.

The Service Link uses TLS encryption in transit. For environments that require egress filtering, allow outbound HTTPS (TCP 443) only to the specific AWS endpoints listed in the Outposts documentation. Do not route Service Link traffic through an untrusted proxy.
IAM and resource policies unchanged.

IAM policies, VPC security groups, NACLs, and resource-based policies work identically on Outposts as in the region. There is no need to relax security controls because the hardware is on-premises — maintain the same least-privilege IAM posture.
S3 on Outposts: Local Object Storage

Standard Amazon S3 stores objects in the AWS Region, not on the Outpost. For applications that require object storage to remain on-premises — large video files, medical imaging archives, or manufacturing sensor data —
S3 on Outposts

provides S3-compatible object storage backed by capacity on the Outpost itself. Data never leaves your premises. You create an S3 on Outposts bucket, an S3 access point tied to a VPC subnet on the Outpost, and use the standard S3 API with a custom endpoint:

# 1. Create an S3 on Outposts bucket (capacity must be provisioned on the Outpost)
aws s3control create-bucket \
  --bucket my-local-archive \
  --outpost-id op-0123456789abcdef0 \
  --create-bucket-configuration LocationConstraint=us-east-1

# 2. Create a VPC access point to the Outpost bucket
aws s3control create-access-point \
  --account-id 111122223333 \
  --name local-access-point \
  --bucket arn:aws:s3-outposts:us-east-1:111122223333:outpost/op-0123456789abcdef0/bucket/my-local-archive \
  --vpc-configuration VpcId=vpc-0a1b2c3d

# 3. Upload an object using the S3 on Outposts endpoint (stays on-premises)
AP_ARN="arn:aws:s3-outposts:us-east-1:111122223333:outpost/op-0123456789abcdef0/accesspoint/local-access-point"
aws s3api put-object \
  --bucket "$AP_ARN" \
  --key patient-scan-2025.dcm \
  --body /data/patient-scan-2025.dcm \
  --endpoint-url https://s3-outposts.us-east-1.amazonaws.com

S3 on Outposts supports versioning, object tagging, and lifecycle policies (to transition or expire objects) but does not support Cross-Region Replication or S3 Intelligent-Tiering. Objects stored in S3 on Outposts count toward the provisioned storage capacity of the Outpost rack.
Capacity Planning for Outposts
Because Outposts capacity is fixed at provisioning time, planning ahead matters more than in the public cloud. Key principles for sizing an Outpost:
Include headroom for failures.

Provision at least 20–30% more capacity than your expected peak. If a physical node in the Outpost fails, AWS replaces it — but during the window before replacement, your remaining nodes must carry the full workload.
Use EC2 capacity reservations.
Reserve specific instance types and counts at the Outpost to guarantee that Auto Scaling can launch instances without contention from other workloads sharing the Outpost.
Burst to the region for spikes.

Architect workloads to tolerate running a portion in the region during traffic spikes that exceed Outpost capacity. The same AMIs and launch templates work in both environments; Auto Scaling can be configured with mixed instance policies that prefer the Outpost subnet and fall back to region subnets.
Monitor capacity proactively.
CloudWatch provides the
OutpostAvailableCapacity
metric for Outpost servers. Set alarms at 70% capacity utilization to trigger the procurement process for additional Outpost capacity before exhaustion — procurement and delivery take weeks.
Key Takeaways
Private cloud is defined by single tenancy and dedicated hardware. AWS delivers this through Outposts (on-premises), Dedicated Hosts (in AWS regions), Dedicated Instances, and GovCloud.
AWS Outposts is the primary private cloud option: AWS-owned hardware in your facility, running the same EC2/ECS/EKS/RDS APIs as the public cloud, with data staying on your premises.
Data traffic never crosses the Service Link — it stays local. Only control-plane operations (launching instances, changing configurations) require the connection to the home AWS Region.

Private cloud adds real cost and complexity: hardware commitments, site requirements, fixed capacity, and a smaller service catalog. Choose it only when a specific regulatory, latency, or licensing requirement justifies those trade-offs.

Most workloads that teams think require private cloud can run in a public cloud Region or with Dedicated Instances — always validate the actual regulatory text before investing in on-premises Outposts.
